summary: The five miracles continue: discussion of the incense vessel being exempt from impurity despite touching the floor; the altar smoke never displaced by wind is elaborated. The Gemara then examines the five things missing from the Second Temple vs. the First: the Ark, the fire from heaven, the divine presence (Shechina), the holy spirit (prophecy), and the Urim VeTumim. The page closes with a discussion of whether the Second Temple altar smoke also rose straight, leading into the arrangement of the altar fire.

display.whats: The ketoret (incense) vessel: does it contract impurity when set on the floor? The Gemara argues it does not because it was designed for a supernatural purpose (the KG carried it into the Holy of Holies where normal space rules did not apply). Then: five things from the First Temple were absent in the Second Temple - the Ark, the heavenly fire, the Shechina, the holy spirit (prophecy), and the Urim VeTumim.
display.hint: The five missing elements from the Second Temple represent the gradual withdrawal of divine immanence. The Ark, fire, and Shechina represent God's direct presence; the holy spirit and Urim VeTumim represent his direct communication. The Second Temple had the structure but not the living divine core.

learning.summary: The ketoret vessel was exempt from impurity transmitted by floor contact, because it served a miraculous purpose beyond normal halachic categories. Five things were present in the First Temple but absent from the Second: the Ark with the tablets and its cover; the heavenly fire that consumed offerings; the Shechina; the holy spirit (prophecy); and the Urim VeTumim that gave oracular responses.
learning.ahaMoment: The five absences from the Second Temple form a devastating list: the physical throne of God (Ark), his direct validation (fire), his presence (Shechina), his communication (prophecy), and his oracle (Urim VeTumim). The Second Temple was magnificent but functionally the divine core was gone.
learning.memoryAnchor: Five things missing from the Second Temple: Ark - fire - Shechina - prophecy - Urim VeTumim.
learning.openQuestion: If the Shechina was absent, in what sense did the Second Temple prayers 'work'? What was accepted?

argumentFlow.label: Does the ketoret vessel contract impurity from floor contact?
argumentFlow.text: A vessel designed to rest on the ground contracts impurity from the ground. The ketoret vessel touched the floor in the Holy of Holies. Does it therefore contract impurity?

argumentFlow.label: It is exempt - it served a miraculous purpose
argumentFlow.text: The ketoret vessel was placed in a space governed by supernatural rules (the Holy of Holies where the Ark occupied no space). Normal impurity rules for floor-contact vessels do not apply.

argumentFlow.label: Five things present in First Temple but absent in Second
argumentFlow.text: The five absences: (1) Ark with tablets and kapporet; (2) heavenly fire; (3) Shechina; (4) holy spirit/prophecy; (5) Urim VeTumim.

theological: what was lost when the First Temple was destroyed

misconceptions: The five missing elements were not merely sentimental losses. They were functional: the fire validated offerings, the Urim gave guidance, the Shechina made the space holy. Without them, the Second Temple had different theological standing.

The Ark's absence means not just the tablets but also the cover (kapporet) which was the site of the Yom Kippur blood sprinkling. The KG in the Second Temple sprinkled on the even hashtiya (foundation stone) instead.

visualizableElements.description: First Temple vs. Second Temple: five elements present vs. absent, shown as a side-by-side comparison
The Ark of the Covenant with its cherubs and kapporet - the central absent element of the Second Temple

quizSeeds.q: What were the five things present in the First Temple that were absent from the Second Temple?
quizSeeds.a: (1) The Ark with the tablets and kapporet; (2) heavenly fire consuming the offerings; (3) the Shechina (divine presence); (4) the holy spirit/prophecy; (5) the Urim VeTumim oracle.

quizSeeds.q: Why was the ketoret vessel considered exempt from floor-contact impurity?
quizSeeds.a: Because it served in the miraculous space of the Holy of Holies, where normal physical rules were suspended (as evidenced by the Ark occupying no measurable space). Normal impurity transmission from floor contact did not apply.

sugyot.finalRuling: Five elements present in the First Temple were absent from the Second. The ketoret vessel was exempt from floor-contact impurity.

glossary.en: The divine presence; God's immanent manifestation in the Temple. One of the five elements absent from the Second Temple.
The oracular device in the High Priest's breastplate that gave divine answers to national questions. Absent from the Second Temple.
The golden cover of the Ark of the Covenant; the Yom Kippur blood was sprinkled there. Absent from the Second Temple.

display.whats: Returning to the fifth miracle from 21a: the smoke of the altar was never displaced by wind. The Gemara examines whether this miracle applied to the Second Temple also, and introduces a dispute: was the smoke from the Second Temple's altar also miraculous, or did it behave normally? This raises the question of whether Second Temple offerings were accepted without the Shechina present.
display.hint: The smoke miracle is not just aesthetics - a smoke column that rises straight signals divine acceptance. In the First Temple this was constant; the debate about the Second Temple reflects the theological uncertainty about whether the Second Temple achieved the same divine ratification.

learning.summary: The smoke miracle (fifth from 21a): the altar smoke rose straight up regardless of wind. Debate: did the Second Temple also have this miracle? The Gemara brings evidence from the Shechina's absence - the Second Temple's smoke behaved differently, confirming the five absences. Some hold the miracle was partial in the Second Temple.
learning.ahaMoment: The smoke's direction was a sign - straight up meant divine acceptance, horizontal or windswept might signal rejection. The First Temple smoke was always straight; the Second Temple's status was ambiguous, reflecting its diminished divine presence.
learning.memoryAnchor: Straight smoke = divine acceptance. First Temple: always straight. Second Temple: debate.
learning.openQuestion: If the Second Temple smoke was not miraculous, were the offerings still accepted by God? What was the status of Second Temple avodah?

argumentFlow.label: Did the Second Temple also have the straight-smoke miracle?
argumentFlow.text: The fifth miracle (straight smoke) was confirmed for the First Temple. Did it also apply to the Second Temple, which lacked the Shechina?

argumentFlow.label: Evidence from the absence of Shechina
argumentFlow.text: Since the Shechina was one of the five absent elements, some argue the smoke miracle also did not apply to the Second Temple.

argumentFlow.label: Alternative: the miracle was partial
argumentFlow.text: Others argue the smoke did rise miraculously in the Second Temple as well - the Shechina's absence did not remove all miraculous dimensions.

argumentFlow.label: The wind and the smoke
argumentFlow.text: The miracle was that even in strong winds the smoke went straight up, not blown sideways. This was supernatural by any physical standard.

argumentFlow.label: Elaboration on the smoke direction
argumentFlow.text: Further analysis of what 'wind cannot displace the smoke' means technically - the smoke was not affected even by a strong east wind.

argumentFlow.label: Conclusion on the smoke miracle
argumentFlow.text: The smoke miracle is considered evidence of divine acceptance. The Gemara closes this discussion before moving to the next topic.

misconceptions: The question is not whether the Second Temple was valid - it was. The question is whether it retained all the miraculous features of the First Temple.
The smoke going straight up is not merely symbolic. In ancient cultures generally, and in Judaism specifically, the direction of sacrifice smoke was read as divine response.

visualizableElements.description: Two altar diagrams: First Temple smoke rising perfectly straight; Second Temple smoke behavior debated
Wind blowing across the Temple Mount while the altar smoke rises straight upward - defying the wind

quizSeeds.q: What was the miracle of the altar smoke, and did it apply to both Temples?
quizSeeds.a: The altar smoke always rose straight upward regardless of wind direction. It definitively applied to the First Temple; whether it applied to the Second Temple is debated, given the absence of the Shechina.

quizSeeds.q: What does the direction of altar smoke symbolize in Jewish tradition?
quizSeeds.a: Straight smoke rising upward signifies divine acceptance of the offerings. Smoke displaced by wind could suggest otherwise.

sugyot.finalRuling: Debate about the Second Temple smoke miracle; definitively the First Temple had it.

glossary.en: Smoke of the altar arrangement; the column of smoke from burning offerings. Its vertical rise was a sign of divine acceptance.

display.whats: The arrangement (ma'aracha) of wood on the altar: the mishna later will describe how to arrange the wood. Here the Gemara begins discussing the arrangement that was present on the altar during Yom Kippur services. The wood arrangement (ma'aracha gedola) and a secondary arrangement (ma'aracha ketana) for the incense coal, and the debate about whether the daily fire came from heaven or was maintained by priests.
display.hint: The two fire arrangements - the large one for offerings and the small one for incense coals - require precise coordination. The question of whether the fire originally came from heaven (divine) or was set by humans has significant implications for the status of the daily offering.

learning.summary: The altar had two fire arrangements: the large ma'aracha for burning offerings and a smaller one for taking coals for the daily incense. A dispute: the fire on the altar always came originally from heaven (divine fire), but priests were also obligated to add human fire daily (a biblical commandment). Both are required.
learning.ahaMoment: Divine fire from heaven burned the offerings; human fire was also commanded. Both were required. The divine fire sanctified the altar; the human fire fulfilled the commandment. This is why the eternal flame (ner tamid) required both sources.
learning.memoryAnchor: Two fire sources: heaven (divine) + human (obligatory). Ma'aracha gedola for offerings; ketana for incense coals.
learning.openQuestion: If divine fire burns everything perfectly, why does the Torah also require human fire? What is the theological significance of the human contribution?

argumentFlow.label: Two fire arrangements on the altar
argumentFlow.text: The large ma'aracha (arrangement) was for burning the daily and additional offerings. The smaller arrangement provided coals for the incense (ketoret).

argumentFlow.label: Was the original fire divine or human?
argumentFlow.text: The original altar fire descended from heaven (divine fire). But the Torah commands 'you shall kindle fire on the altar' - requiring human fire as well. Both are needed.

argumentFlow.label: Biblical sources for the dual fire requirement
argumentFlow.text: One verse says fire came from God; another says the priests must kindle fire. The resolution: both are required - divine for sanctification, human for commandment fulfillment.

argumentFlow.label: The arrangement of wood on the altar
argumentFlow.text: The wood was arranged specifically: large logs to form the main structure, smaller pieces inside. The arrangement had a specific halakhic form.

argumentFlow.label: The incense coal arrangement
argumentFlow.text: A separate smaller arrangement provided the coals that the KG would carry in the pan into the Holy of Holies on Yom Kippur.

argumentFlow.label: Summary of the fire section
argumentFlow.text: Both the divine fire and human fire were features of the altar service, and the two ma'arachot served distinct halakhic purposes.

theological: human and divine collaboration in worship

misconceptions: The divine fire did not negate the human commandment. Both were required simultaneously: divine fire confirmed divine acceptance; human fire fulfilled the biblical mandate 'you shall kindle.'
The incense coal arrangement (ma'aracha ketana) was not for burning the incense in the Temple - it was specifically for producing coals that the KG would carry into the Holy of Holies on Yom Kippur.

visualizableElements.description: The altar with two distinct fire arrangements labeled: ma'aracha gedola (large, for offerings) and ma'aracha ketana (small, for incense coals)
Divine fire descending from heaven onto the altar while a priest simultaneously adds wood - fulfilling both the divine and human fire requirements

quizSeeds.q: What were the two fire arrangements on the altar and what purpose did each serve?
quizSeeds.a: The ma'aracha gedola (large arrangement) burned the daily and additional offerings. The ma'aracha ketana (small arrangement) produced the coals that the High Priest carried in his pan for the Yom Kippur ketoret service.

quizSeeds.q: Were both divine fire and human fire required on the altar? Why?
quizSeeds.a: Yes. Divine fire descended from heaven (a recurring miracle) for sanctification. Human fire was separately commanded by the Torah ('you shall kindle fire') as a mitzvah. Both were necessary simultaneously.

sugyot.finalRuling: Both divine fire and human fire were required on the altar. Two arrangements served distinct purposes: offerings and incense coals.

glossary.en: The large fire arrangement on the altar for burning offerings.
The small fire arrangement for producing coals for the incense pan.
Divine presence; one of five elements absent from the Second Temple.
The oracular breastplate device; absent from the Second Temple.
The golden cover of the Ark; site of Yom Kippur blood sprinkling; absent from Second Temple.
Large fire arrangement on the altar for burning offerings.
Small fire arrangement producing coals for the incense pan.
Smoke of the altar arrangement; its vertical rise signified divine acceptance.
